I commonly get asked this question by people throughout the DAP, about other people's deep mentorship, and he has a digital agency on him, as a veteran in the space. I want you to understand that this question, should you be transparent? We're going to cover in phase number seven of the team building phase. It's a pretty important question. Throughout my entire business, I've always wondered if I, personally from experience, should either share the amount of revenue that I'm generating from the people in the client's space, or if I should be transparent on operational issues or hiccups that we're having as a business, or things that I need to fix. You see what I didn't learn until about 12 months into my 15 months in this specific business, we've got problems as entrepreneurs will literally never go away. You'll always experience problems. And at one point in my business, when I thought I was going to go out of business, I became transparent the hard way by going to my team and saying, guys, I don't really know what to tell you guys, other than, you've hit a little bit of a hardship. I can't express this enough that as a team, we are here to generate team results. I feel like I've been robbed recently by the people that are within my own organization, because the people who are in my team aren't focused on results or aren't expressing that they're focused on results by their behaviors and actions. I'm watching people in my organization not communicate with one another, not be team oriented, and more importantly, not get results for our clients that keep us in business. Because of this, in January, we had 22 clients. In February, we lost 50%. In March, we lost another 50%. This was the conversation I had with them in March, and I told you the story before. The morning on that Wednesday, when I knew I had to make payroll on Friday to people who had kids and other staff members, I knew on Wednesday morning when I got that plane at 8 a.m. as my tears welled up when I started to cry. That business was just crashing down on me. I had to get help from my own team. I never was that transparent in my business, but I had gone to them and I'll continue the conversation. I said, guys, I need you to step up and focus. We need you guys to all be oriented towards delivering massive results for the people who are giving us the opportunity to deliver them business. We have to focus and drill down and do as much as we possibly can for these individuals because they give us the honor of calling them a client. You see, we've been disrespectful, and I know we've been scattered, and I accept full responsibilities, your executive, for being often euphoria of not being as focused as I should, leading you all to the example that you shouldn't be as focused as I need you to now be. You see, if we don't turn this around, we need you to now be. You see, if we don't turn this around, we need you to be focused on the people who are giving us the honor of calling them a client. In order to overcome this recent copterbands, in order to overcome this recent copterbands, which I'm gonna cover in base seven, the team building base. Which I'm gonna cover in base seven, the team building base. I had massive success the following month. You see in April, which was just last month, I did over $65,000 in revenue in Megalodon and over another $20,000 in the Jeremy Haines personality break. Just four months ago, where the business, just four months ago, where the business was at its peak. Now in May, as I've discussed, we're now at that $110,000 mark. So see, once again, another number significantly above, in which we had hit in that January month, up to only nine clients comparatively, on the same amount of staff members. What had happened to my team in that month is the same thing that happened to me. I collectively made a crisis, and I took advantage of the crisis to manipulate the keystone habits of my organization, which are our core habits or beliefs, in order to overcome a productivity barrier that had been built. See, in the habits of, I'm sorry, in the power habits by Charles Duhigg, Charles Duhigg talks about in chapter seven, a gentleman named Neil, Neil Sutton. Neil, Paul O'Neill, I'm sorry, so Paul O'Neill, Paul O'Neill was the CEO. He was an executive on Coca-Cola, and then he went over and he became the CEO of Alcoa Aluma. Now Alcoa at the time was one of the worst rated companies for safety standards in history, having people die inside of their plan on a frequent basis. Now, although this at the time was almost a standard around the entire world, they had the worst reputation for safety inside their organization. Now, obviously all their shareholders as a publicly traded company weren't really liking the direction they were going, but with the new CEO, Paul O'Neill, coming on, they had a hope that there would be a turnaround within the organization. Paul gets up on stage at a shareholders meeting, and it's the very first time he's gonna present a room full of people who invest almost, if not all, their money into this organization as it was a massive operation. There's stockbrokers in the room, there's obviously shareholders, and there's people who are just there in the spectacle wondering what was gonna happen. Now Paul gets up on stage, and the only thing that he focuses on is safety standards. The only thing that he talked about was the safety that he was going to implement in the company, and some of the processes and systems that they were gonna follow in order to overcome these cumbersome barriers that became an internal standard for safety issues. Shareholders and stockbrokers literally were sprinting out of the room by the dozen calling people to sell off the stock before everybody else in the room left to sell off theirs, to try not to lose as much money as they thought they would if they continued to hold their value in that stock. Paul O'Neill just months later, after implementing all the systems, processes, and procedures into this business, had a complete turnaround, and not just people stopping death and dying inside of his organization. He had an increase in rapport, in morals, in values, and in overall company culture with the productivity going through the room causing a spike in their revenue they had never seen before, raising them above the highest points that they've ever had on the New York Stock Exchange. I give you this story and then talk about it, because in this specific video of being transparent and giving you lessons about what you can do as an executive, you have the ability to sometimes take problems that you could or could not be transparent about to create a crisis, to manipulate the keystone habit of an organization of a small and large like Alcoa like Paul O'Neill was able to manipulate during a time by just pointing out safety standards, he created a crisis where he manipulated the crisis into an opportunity state where he then took that opportunity state throughout his entire company to change everybody's behaviors. Behaviors and actions are some of the hardest things to change and manage otherwise manipulate inside an organization as a leader. You have to be able to create and identify crises inside of your organization and when you can create a crisis in order to open up an area for opportunity to manipulate the keystone habits of your organization for success in higher levels of productivity. You see several times throughout my organization the history of it, I've almost looked at the problems that are within it, which are always gonna be there as I've identified at the beginning part of this video, pointing them out directly, identified them as a crisis, made it into a bigger problem than it was, caused a fire, shook the fire, made it bigger, and then everybody starts getting a little shaky, get a little anxious, and they start moving a little faster, they start getting in that state where they know they gotta get something done and put out a fire, right? What you difference in behavior operate differently, and move differently, you gotta create crises occasionally in your business through being transparent in order to manipulate the keystone habits of your organization for success. I've been transparent a few times. It's always been successful in my business. It doesn't mean that I'm going out of my way to constantly share all the deals that we're doing now. I do, but I also justify the fact that I'm massive expenses so people don't feel like they need to ask for raises when they hear we're doing 25K or 100K deals now. They just simply acknowledge here and understand that there's been a large deal that we celebrate the success of internally because at one point we had those money issues that we were talking about that I was transparent with. So in the long term, I imagine that people will come to me after we have more success and ask for raises, but I'm also gonna be transparent with them at that time, and I will be in a state where it will be available, if you will, then become to me and ask those questions, because there's different times where they will be able to see when it's appropriate to do so or not. They know not to ask those questions right now, you know? The moral of the story is when you're transparent, you get different opportunities that aren't there if you aren't looking for different ways of how you can use the transparency to leverage into additional productivity, efficiency, or higher standards throughout your organization. This is why you should be transparent based on the team building, guys. I'll see you in the next video.
